metadata.title: Chapter 1: Pipeline Architecture & Service Catalog
metadata.course_name: IO-107: SDLC Pipeline and Deployment Guardrails

slides.subtitle:
Chapter 1:
Pipeline Architecture and Service Catalog
slides.title: IO-107: SDLC Pipeline and Deployment Guardrails
Chapter Objectives
bullets: In this chapter, you will:
Describe how a Terraform change flows from a Service Catalog product through the pipeline to AWS
Name the orchestration tools — Jenkins, CloudBees, AWS CodePipeline, CodeDeploy — and what each does
Identify the standard pipeline stages and where validation happens
List the primary deployment targets: EKS, Lambda, and Aurora

rows: Service Catalog as the Source of Approved Terraform
Orchestration Tool Mix and Pipeline Stages

slides.title: Why Pipeline-Driven
bullets: Audit trail: every change ties back to a Git commit, PR review, and pipeline execution
Consistency: the same approved Terraform produces the same infrastructure every time
Approval gates: humans review production changes; the pipeline enforces the gate
Compliance baked in: encryption, tagging, naming, and network rules checked before deploy
slides.speaker_notes: Frame this as the audit story — when an auditor or incident-responder asks 'who changed this, when, and who approved it?' we point at the pipeline. Manual console clicks don't give us that. One prerequisite to call out once: everything downstream of `git push` is only as trustworthy as the commit on `main`. The standard pattern requires (a) branch protection with required reviews on `main`, (b) 2FA on every contributor account, and (c) signed commits on production-targeted repos. These source-control controls are out of scope for this course but every diagram we cover today assumes they're in place — pipeline integrity collapses without them.

rows: Service Catalog as the Source of Approved Terraform
Orchestration Tool Mix and Pipeline Stages

slides.title: AWS Service Catalog
bullets: Service Catalog products are pre-approved Terraform modules
Cloud platform team authors and versions the products
Developers consume products — they don't author raw Terraform
Product version pins which AWS resources, settings, and tags are allowed
slides.speaker_notes: This is the piece that's specific to this environment: you don't write Terraform from scratch — you provision from a Service Catalog product. There's a product for an S3 bucket with encryption and BPA baked in, a product for an EKS namespace with IRSA wired up, a product for an Aurora cluster with the right parameter groups and KMS keys. You supply parameters (env, app name, owner, cost centre) and the product expands to compliant Terraform. By the time your change reaches OPA validation, it's already structurally compliant — Service Catalog is the *first* guardrail, before the pipeline even runs.

slides.title: From Service Catalog to a Deployed Workload

rows: Developer picks Service Catalog product + parameters
Product generates compliant HCL into the app's repo
Build runs `terraform plan`; OPA checks rules
Human approves in CodePipeline / Jenkins / CloudBees

slides.speaker_notes: Walk this top to bottom. The developer never types raw Terraform from scratch — they parameterise an approved product. The pipeline picks up the resulting commit, plans, validates, gates, and deploys. Cross-account role assumption is how the pipeline (running in a shared tools account) can land changes in dev/stg/prd accounts without persistent credentials. We'll spend Module 6 on the OPA step and Module 7 on what the cross-account / SCP layer looks like.

rows: Service Catalog as the Source of Approved Terraform
Orchestration Tool Mix and Pipeline Stages

slides.title: Pipeline Tool Mix

rows: Long-standing CI engine — builds, tests, triggers
Enterprise Jenkins — RBAC, audit, shared libraries
AWS-native orchestrator for newer cloud workloads
Deploy step — traffic shifting, blue/green, in-place

slides.speaker_notes: The enterprise CI/CD platform doesn't use one CI/CD tool — it uses a mix. Jenkins has been the workhorse for years and runs many existing pipelines. CloudBees is the enterprise Jenkins distribution that adds RBAC, audit, and shared libraries — that's the central control plane the platform team manages. AWS CodePipeline is the AWS-native orchestrator newer EKS and Lambda workloads tend to use. AWS CodeDeploy is the deployment executor — handling Lambda alias traffic shifting, EC2 rolling updates, ECS blue/green. We focus on the AWS-native path today because that's where modernisation is happening, but you'll encounter Jenkins/CloudBees pipelines too. You don't need to administer any of these — just know which one you're looking at when something fails.

slides.title: AWS CodePipeline — the AWS-Native Orchestrator
bullets: Event-driven: Amazon EventBridge triggers the pipeline on a Git change
Stages contain actions; artifacts pass from one stage to the next
Built-in execution history and audit trail
Calls CodeBuild for build/test, CodeDeploy for deploy, custom actions for OPA
slides.speaker_notes: EventBridge — you may still see the older name 'CloudWatch Events' in legacy docs and API responses, it's the same service rebranded in 2019 — detects the commit and kicks off the pipeline. The pipeline executes a series of stages; the output of one stage passes to the next as an artifact in S3. CodePipeline doesn't do the work itself — it coordinates CodeBuild for build/test, CodeDeploy for deploy, and custom actions for things like OPA evaluation.

slides.title: AWS CodeBuild — Where Work Happens

rows: Defines install / pre_build / build / post_build phases
Runs your commands in a clean, reproducible env
Joins your VPC to reach private EKS / Aurora resources
All stdout/stderr captured — start troubleshooting here
Terraform plan, Docker image, Lambda zip to next

slides.speaker_notes: CodeBuild is where execution happens. buildspec.yml defines phases — install, pre_build, build, post_build — each running commands in sequence in a container. Critical capability: CodeBuild can join your VPC, essential for reaching private EKS clusters or running Aurora migrations against private subnets. All output goes to CloudWatch Logs — that's where you start troubleshooting when something fails. Artifacts from build (Terraform plan, container image, Lambda zip) are handed to the next pipeline stage in S3.

slides.title: Pipeline Stages

rows: tf apply, helm upgrade, sam deploy

slides.speaker_notes: Walk through each stage. Source triggers on commit. Build runs terraform plan for infra or compiles code for apps. Policy validation is where OPA evaluates the plan against the rules — we spend all of Module 6 on this. Approval is the manual gate for stg/prd. Deploy is where it actually happens. A failure at any stage stops the pipeline immediately — you don't waste approval cycles on a change that failed OPA, and you don't waste deploy windows on a change that failed the build.

rows: Service Catalog as the Source of Approved Terraform
Orchestration Tool Mix and Pipeline Stages

slides.title: Primary Deployment Targets
slides.speaker_notes: Three primary targets you'll see all day. EKS is the container platform — Module 3 covers Helm-based deployment, kubectl, and IRSA. Lambda is the serverless target — Module 4 covers SAM, versioning, aliases, and CodeDeploy-driven traffic shifting. Aurora/RDS is for databases — Module 5 covers schema migrations and Aurora Blue/Green via Terraform, which Lab 4 exercises. One acknowledgement: EC2 still exists for legacy workloads, and we are actively migrating those to EKS or Lambda. This course concentrates on the cloud-native targets because that's where new work is heading.

slides.title: Chapter Summary
bullets: In this chapter, you have:
Described how Service Catalog → Terraform → Git → pipeline → AWS works
Named the orchestration tools: Jenkins, CloudBees, AWS CodePipeline, AWS CodeDeploy
Identified the standard pipeline stages and where OPA, approval, and deploy sit
Listed the primary deployment targets: EKS, Lambda, and Aurora
